title: Muhammara
subtitle: Syrian roasted red pepper and walnut dip
excerpt: Roasted red peppers blended with toasted walnuts, pomegranate molasses, and cumin into a thick, sweet-sour dip with a gentle heat. The best thing to put out alongside hummus. Serves four to six as a starter.
sourceNotes: Muhammara originates in Aleppo, Syria, where the local Aleppo pepper and pomegranate molasses define its character. The dip is documented by Anissa Helou in Feast: Food of the Islamic World (2018) and by Claudia Roden in A New Book of Middle Eastern Food (1968). Outside Syria, Aleppo pepper is often substituted with a combination of mild chilli flakes and sweet paprika.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Keeps refrigerated for up to five days.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Improves with a few hours in the fridge — the flavours meld as it sits. Make it the morning of serving for the best result.

content.text: Muhammara uses the same walnut-pomegranate pairing as khoresh fesenjan but in a completely different register: cold, blended, bright, and fast to make. The jarred roasted red peppers in olive oil that most supermarkets stock work very well here. If you want to roast your own, grill them under a high heat until blackened, then steam in a covered bowl for 10 minutes and peel.

items.prepNote: roasted and peeled, or from a jar (drained weight about 250 g)

content.text: Toast the walnuts in a dry frying pan over medium heat for 3–4 minutes until they smell nutty and are lightly coloured. Let them cool before blending — hot walnuts make the oil in the dip separate.

Put the roasted peppers, cooled walnuts, {{breadcrumbs-dried}} of breadcrumbs, {{pomegranate-molasses}} of pomegranate molasses, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes in a food processor. Blitz to a rough paste — the texture should have some character, not be completely smooth. Add {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil, the lemon juice, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Blitz briefly to combine. Taste and adjust: more pomegranate molasses for sourness, more chilli for heat, more salt if flat. Spoon into a shallow bowl, drizzle with a little more olive oil, and serve with warm flatbread or raw vegetables.

items.cause: Peppers not well drained, or too much olive oil.
items.fix: Drain jarred peppers thoroughly. Add the olive oil last, a tablespoon at a time, stopping when the texture is right — it should mound on a spoon.

items.cause: Not enough pomegranate molasses or lemon.
items.fix: Add another half tablespoon of pomegranate molasses and another squeeze of lemon. The dip needs an acidic edge to balance the sweetness of the peppers.
Always let toasted walnuts cool completely before blending. The emulsion holds better with cold walnuts.

content.text: Muhammara comes from Aleppo, the ancient city in northern Syria that gave its name to the mild, fruity dried chilli pepper that defines the dish in its original form. Aleppo pepper has a moderate heat and a slightly oily, complex flavour that is different from standard chilli flakes. Outside the Middle East, the standard substitution is a mix of sweet paprika and mild chilli flakes, which approximates the character. The dip is part of the Syrian meze tradition and has spread through the diaspora into the wider consciousness of Middle Eastern food.
